conclusion: redb 4.1.0 README and 196 passing upstream library/integration tests prove a pure-Rust embedded key-value library with no database daemon or sidecar. This verdict is about redb itself, not the larger dynamically linked LifeOS closure.

Current pinned redb 4.1.0 removed its mmap backend. Its optimized file backend uses positioned file reads/writes, and Database exposes a configurable userspace cache (default 1 GiB). The report's current mmap and operating-system-cache-bypass statement is false.

redb documents a zero-copy, BTreeMap-based API and can return borrowed views, but its Value trait explicitly serializes with as_bytes and deserializes with from_bytes. Storage uses copy-on-write B-trees, not std::collections::BTreeMap, so 'native Rust types without serialization' is too broad.

redb 4.1.0 documents full ACID transactions and serializable isolation; the complete local redb test suite passed 196 tests including immediate persistence, read isolation, transaction behavior, recovery-sensitive paths, and multithreading.

redb 4.1.0 documents MVCC with one writer and multiple concurrent snapshot readers; design documentation and passing concurrency tests prove readers do not block the active writer. Writer-to-writer concurrency remains serialized.
The pinned artifact is a Rust library crate linked into applications. It is not a compiler and exposes embedded database APIs.

redb's Rust implementation is compatible with a musl/static target in principle, but portability is a whole-closure property. The active CodeDB/Yazelix binaries are dynamically linked glibc ELF files with Nix-store interpreter/RUNPATH entries, and the effective Rust toolchain lacked the musl std target, so the claimed portable closure is not currently proven.

redb 4.1.0 is a transactional key-value B-tree engine. It exposes no vector coordinate, geometry, nearest-neighbor, HNSW, or distance-computation API. The current Bun-loaded ruvector Node package owns vector geometry.
redb implements no cosine-similarity operation. Cosine is supplied by application or RuVector code; the Bun-loaded ruvector 0.2.34 package exports cosineSimilarity and native vector database APIs.

The exact zero-copy-f32-plus-AVX-512 redb path does not exist in redb 4.1.0. A reproducible benchmark measured redb byte reads and commits plus a separate scalar application cosine loop at 384 and 4096 dimensions; microsecond results do not convert that application loop into redb functionality or prove AVX-512 use.

The component boundary is correct: embedding models create semantic vectors while redb passively stores and retrieves caller-supplied encoded values. Distance computation and semantic meaning remain external.
redb is an in-process single-node embedded store and exposes no network server, replication protocol, or distributed-consensus API. Distribution therefore belongs to application protocols.

redb is key-value and has no SQL or automatic relational secondary-index facility. Qualification is required because it supports named tables, range scans, and multimap tables from which an application can explicitly build indexes.

The local benchmark proves low redb latency on this workstation (1536-byte read p50 about 0.27 microseconds; Immediate commit p50 about 20-24 microseconds), but no claim-scoped LMDB comparator, edge-node hardware run, determinism definition, or crash/sync matrix exists. Competitive-with-LMDB remains unproved.

redb can technically implement an embedded high-frequency scratchpad, and measured local operations are fast enough for that use class. No accepted LifeOS data-class, retention, privacy, promotion, or recovery decision makes this its current exclusive role.

redb can store an embedding under a BLAKE3-derived key, so the mechanism is feasible. No current model-aware cache schema or implementation proves that content, model, version, configuration, invalidation, and collision handling are all covered.

redb can back a durable application outbox or replay queue, but it is not PostgreSQL WAL and no current ordered/idempotent redb-to-PostgreSQL outage protocol is implemented. WAL, outbox, queue, buffer, and authority semantics must not be conflated.

Current CodeDB Rust code can transform supported source files into structured syntax rows and Nushell can host those rows. It is not a universal Nushell-owned AST transformer: language coverage varies and Cargo dependency rows come from cargo metadata rather than the Rust AST.

At canonical nu_plugin origin/master 9451b20, the Rust plugin uses the Nushell MessagePack protocol but returns typed table values and has no direct redb dependency. Durable redb capture is a separate codedb CLI/BlobStore path, so no direct plugin-MessagePack-to-redb stream exists.

An intermediate redb ingestion buffer is a technically possible target, but it is not the universal current role. Current CodeDB uses redb as a durable default source-blob store and envctl uses redb separately for migration/state; authority must be decided per data class.

envctl commit c911286 reads exported JSONL rows that already contain embedding_minilm vectors into an in-memory RuVector index. It neither pulls CodeDB redb blocks, generates MiniLM embeddings, nor commits code and vectors to PostgreSQL.

Reproducible local evidence supports the redb microsecond tier for the measured byte-read and Immediate-commit operations. No equivalent PostgreSQL-plus-RuVector relational/GNN benchmark was run, and Bun-loaded ruvector itself exposes native sub-millisecond vector operations, so the report's global microsecond-versus-millisecond boundary is not established.

RuVector includes a native pgrx PostgreSQL extension that installs with CREATE EXTENSION and needs no vector-database sidecar. RuVector is broader than that extension: the current developer-facing ruvector 0.2.34 Node package loaded a standalone native N-API vector database through Bun, and no installed LifeOS PostgreSQL extension activation was proven.

Current envctl does not select PostgreSQL branch state or reconstruct files from module_path. Its optional semantic adapter treats module_path as vector identity/metadata; current materialization is a separate CodeDB operation.

Nix/Yazelix can compile a source directory and CodeDB can separately materialize stored files, but no current end-to-end selected-PostgreSQL-state to materialized directory to isolated Yazelix/Nix build trace exists.
redb is passive transactional storage and has no ranking or learning semantics. Any ranking change is owned by application, model, RuVector, SONA, or AgentDB code.

The latest developer-facing AgentDB package proves the core integration: agentdb 3.0.0-alpha.17 exposes SelfLearningRvfBackend over .rvf, wraps @ruvector/sona, enhances searches, records trajectories, accepts quality feedback, trains patterns, and a Bun live probe created an AgentDB .rvf plus native SONA learning. Qualification is required because search does not expose the internal q_* trajectory ID expected by recordFeedback; the documented hit-ID call recorded zero trajectories in the live probe, so the complete feedback-to-ranking contract is currently defective.

The latest Bun-loaded packages prove every narrower component: RVF defines model/overlay/LoRA segments, native SONA exposes Micro-LoRA, ruvllm held and switched 51 in-memory LoRA adapters, and Tiny Dancer trained and scored a FastGRNN router. They do not prove that one current AgentDB .rvf file persisted both the Micro-LoRA adapters and FastGRNN model, nor that 50+ complete ruvllm personalities hot-swap within one measured hardware footprint.

The defensible boundary is verified: redb can be one portable embedded storage component but is not a geometry engine, vector database, learning system, SQL authority, or complete intelligence architecture. Its elevation to the strategic LifeOS foundation remains an unrecorded owner architecture decision.
